hero.title: Live cue cards, routed only through your NullHub
hero.sub: NullCap is a native desktop overlay for macOS, Windows and Linux that shows short answer cards on your screen during interviews, meetings, sales calls and demos. It captures screen and audio and transcribes speech locally, then sends every AI request to exactly one service: NullHub, a gateway running on your own machine at 127.0.0.1:19800 by default.

primary.label: Build from source (Zig 0.16.0)
primary.code:
zig build
zig build run -- doctor

quickstart:
NullCap expects a running NullHub at `http://127.0.0.1:19800`. From the repo root:

```bash
# check environment, permissions and NullHub reachability
zig build run -- doctor

# first-run bootstrap: privacy preset, seed workflow profiles
zig build run -- onboarding apply

# pick a session mode
zig build run -- mode meeting

# start the native overlay with global hotkeys
zig build run -- run
```

features.title: One gateway, zero keys
features.body: Every request — text, image or audio — goes to exactly one address: your local NullHub gateway, 127.0.0.1:19800 by default. NullCap stores no provider API keys and never contacts a model or speech-to-text provider itself. Four cloud providers — Gemini, Groq, OpenAI and Claude — sit behind the gateway, which decides which one answers; their keys stay in NullHub, never in the app.

features.title: Live cue cards, seven modes
features.body: Short on-screen answers, structures, follow-ups and code hints while a call runs, drawn from screen captures and transcribed audio. Seven session modes — interview, meeting, demo, sales, debug, system-design, general — reshape every cue and post-call artifact, and the cue/ask command exposes 21 prompt modes for finer control. Text captured from your screen is treated as untrusted evidence, never as instructions to the model.

features.title: Local speech-to-text
features.body: Speech is transcribed on your machine with local ONNX models — 15 in the catalog, from the Moonshine and Whisper/distil-whisper families. Your microphone is labelled "me", system audio — the other side of the call — is labelled "other". Voice-activity detection drops clearly silent chunks before anything is transcribed, and long calls compact into rolling local summaries.

features.title: Hidden from screen shares
features.body: The overlay asks the OS to exclude it from capture — NSWindowSharingNone on macOS, SetWindowDisplayAffinity WDA_EXCLUDEFROMCAPTURE on Windows — so it does not appear in Zoom, Meet, Teams or OBS shares. This is a presentation convenience, not a proctoring bypass: it does not defeat proctoring software, kernel capture drivers or HDMI capture cards.

features.title: Phone mirror and a second operator
features.body: An optional HTTP server (default port 4123) mirrors live status and controls to your phone, gated by a 24-byte pairing token compared in constant time; it binds to 127.0.0.1 unless you pass --lan. A separate duo mode lets an invited helper send live tips during a call through a consent gate.

features.title: Post-call artifacts, local-first
features.body: Local summaries, action items, follow-up email drafts and mode-specific scorecards. Tasks export as text, JSON, Jira, Linear or Asana. Sessions, chats, transcripts and notes live under ~/.nullcap with opt-in persistence, retention windows and purge commands. No provider API keys are stored, ever.

note: Experimental: no tagged releases and no license file yet — build from source and expect change.

plain.what: NullCap is a native desktop overlay app, written in Zig with hand-written windowing code for each OS — Objective-C on macOS, C on Windows and Linux — that floats above your call and shows short AI cue cards: answers, structures, follow-up questions, code hints. It captures screen, microphone and system audio locally, transcribes speech on-device with ONNX Whisper and Moonshine models, and keeps a live transcript in memory. Around the overlay sits a batch-mode CLI of roughly 90 subcommands covering setup, diagnostics, privacy controls, post-call artifacts and job-search coaching.
plain.why: Alt-tabbing to a chatbot mid-call is slow, and it hands your conversation to whichever cloud service that chatbot uses. NullCap keeps transcription on your machine and sends every AI request to exactly one address — your local NullHub gateway (the Null ecosystem's model router, a companion app you run yourself) at 127.0.0.1:19800 — never to OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini or Groq directly. The app stores zero provider API keys; the gateway holds them and decides which model answers each request.
plain.when: Before a job interview, customer demo or sales call: start a NullHub gateway, run the overlay, and pick one of seven session modes — the default is interview. During the call you get on-screen cues drawn from what it sees and hears; afterwards you get a local summary, action items, a mode-specific scorecard and a follow-up email draft, with tasks exportable to Jira, Linear or Asana.
plain.fit: The specialist — a single-purpose desktop copilot for live calls, deliberately outside the server stack; every AI call goes through your own NullHub.

how.intro: NullCap has no tagged releases yet, so the path is build-from-source with Zig 0.16.0 (or the Nix flake). Everything below assumes one prerequisite: a NullHub gateway reachable at http://127.0.0.1:19800.

steps.title: Build it
steps.body: Clone the repo and build. There are zero third-party dependencies — every import is the Zig standard library or local code — so the build is just the compiler. On macOS, the app-bundle target also writes an Info.plist with the microphone and screen-recording usage strings and ad-hoc codesigns with a pinned identifier, so the OS-level screen and microphone permission grants survive rebuilds.

steps.title: Run the self-test
steps.body: doctor checks your environment end to end: OS permissions for screen and microphone capture, the local speech-to-text model directory, and whether a NullHub is actually answering at the configured URL. If your gateway lives somewhere else, point the app at it once with config set-nullhub — the URL is validated client-side (http/https only, no userinfo, no path traversal).

steps.title: Apply first-run defaults
steps.body: onboarding apply applies the balanced privacy preset and seeds workflow profiles for the session modes. Privacy presets — strict, balanced, memory, open — control what persists under ~/.nullcap and for how long; switch later with the privacy preset command. Nothing about this step touches the network beyond your gateway.

steps.title: Pick a session mode
steps.body: Seven modes — interview, meeting, demo, sales, debug, system-design, general — reshape every cue and every post-call artifact. The shipped default is interview, which tells you what this tool was built around; switch it per call.

steps.title: Start the overlay
steps.body: run launches the always-on-top native overlay with global hotkeys and starts the capture lanes you enabled. From here the loop is: audio is transcribed locally, cue cards appear as the call unfolds, and when you hang up the session, transcript and notes are yours to summarize, review or purge.

inside.title: 4 cloud providers behind the gateway, 0 keys in the app
inside.note: Keys are managed in NullHub, never stored client-side; six model families — OpenAI, Gemini Flash, Gemini Pro, Claude, Groq Llama, Local Llama — are classified for tier routing so the gateway can pick fast or careful models per request.

inside.title: 15 local speech-to-text models
inside.note: ONNX models that run on-device; Whisper Tiny through Medium ship in both English-only (.en) and multilingual variants, which is how the catalog reaches 15 entries.

inside.title: 7 session modes, 21 cue prompt modes
inside.note: Each mode reshapes cues and post-call artifacts; the cue/ask command adds 21 prompt flags — --assist, --answer, --structure, --followups, --code-hint, --system-design and more — for one-shot questions outside the live loop.

inside.title: 5 capture lanes
inside.note: Each lane toggles independently via capture enable/disable; mic is labelled "me", system audio — the other side of the call — is labelled "other".

inside.title: 3 hand-written native overlays
inside.note: About 7,200 lines of Objective-C and C, not a shared toolkit — which is what lets each platform ask its OS to exclude the overlay from screen capture.

useCases.title: A mock interview loop that never leaves your laptop
useCases.body: You have a systems-design interview on Friday. Set mode interview (the default), drill questions and answers against the overlay with the mock command, and use the prep, speech and salary commands to work on answers, pacing and negotiation. Audio is transcribed by a local Whisper model, cues come through your own NullHub, and the review command scores a transcript or your notes afterwards — nothing about your practice run is stored with a model provider.
useCases.code:
zig build run -- mode interview
zig build run -- mock question
zig build run -- review --scorecard

useCases.title: Sales calls that end with tickets, not memory
useCases.body: Switch to sales mode for a discovery call. During the call the overlay surfaces structures and follow-up questions from the live transcript; afterwards you get a scorecard, action items and a follow-up email draft, all generated locally through the gateway. Tasks export as text, JSON, or straight into Jira, Linear or Asana.
useCases.code:
zig build run -- mode sales
zig build run -- tasks export --format linear

useCases.title: Your phone as the control surface
useCases.body: You're presenting full-screen and can't touch the overlay. mirror serve starts a small HTTP server (port 4123 by default) that shows live status and controls on your phone, paired with a 24-byte random token compared in constant time and stored 0600 in ~/.nullcap. It binds to 127.0.0.1 unless you explicitly pass --lan, and the token can be rotated at any time.
useCases.code:
zig build run -- mirror serve --port 4123
zig build run -- mirror rotate-token

useCases.title: A trusted colleague on the line, with consent
useCases.body: duo mode lets you invite a second operator — a mentor watching your demo, a senior engineer backing you up — who can push short live tips onto your overlay. Tips only flow after an explicit consent gate on your side, and you can list and revoke helpers at any point.
useCases.code:
zig build run -- duo invite helper@example
zig build run -- duo consent on

faq.q: Does my call audio ever reach a cloud service?
faq.a: Raw audio, no. Speech-to-text runs on-device with local ONNX models, and voice-activity detection drops silent chunks before transcription. Text and screen evidence that need an LLM go to exactly one host — your NullHub gateway at 127.0.0.1:19800 — which then decides whether a cloud provider (Gemini, Groq, OpenAI, Claude) or a local model answers. So transcript excerpts can reach a provider, but only through your gateway and its policy, never from the app directly.

No. The build files pin version 1.0.0, but there are no tagged releases and no license file yet — you build from source and should expect breaking changes. On the plus side, the repo carries 513 test blocks and the code is dependency-free Zig, so "experimental" describes the release process more than the code quality.

faq.q: The overlay hides from screen shares — does that beat proctoring software?
faq.a: No, and it isn't meant to. The overlay uses OS-level capture exclusion (NSWindowSharingNone on macOS, WDA_EXCLUDEFROMCAPTURE on Windows), which keeps it out of Zoom, Meet, Teams and OBS shares. It does nothing against proctoring agents, kernel-level capture drivers or an HDMI capture card. There is also a build-time process-disguise flag that renames the binary to look like Terminal or Activity Monitor; it exists in the tree, and you should check the rules of whatever call you're on before using either feature.

faq.q: What happens if someone puts malicious text on my screen during a call?
faq.a: Screen text and transcripts are modelled as untrusted evidence, not instructions: the assistant snapshot carries ordered trust levels, marks screen/transcript/reference content untrusted, and enables prompt-injection detection and evidence escaping. It's a real, first-class defense — but like all prompt-injection mitigation today, it reduces risk rather than eliminating it.

faq.q: What do I need besides the app?
faq.a: A running NullHub — the Null ecosystem's local gateway that holds provider keys and routes model requests — plus Zig 0.16.0 to build (or Nix). Two more services from the same tool family are wired in with defaults, nulltracker on port 7700 and nullboiler on port 8080, but they're optional integrations, not requirements.

faq.q: Is the phone mirror safe to expose?
faq.a: By default it isn't exposed at all: the server binds 127.0.0.1 and only listens on 0.0.0.0 if you pass --lan. Every route is gated by a 24-byte hex pairing token compared in constant time, stored with 0600 permissions, and rotatable with mirror rotate-token. There's no auth beyond the token, so treat --lan as trusted-network-only.

faq.q: Where does my data live, and can I delete it?
faq.a: Everything is under ~/.nullcap: config.json, session state, transcripts, notes and logs. Persistence is opt-in with retention windows set by privacy presets (strict, balanced, memory, open), and privacy purge expired or purge all removes stored data on demand. No provider API keys are ever written anywhere by the app.
